author: @Meat5000
text: There is a paper on the net with a magnet spin experiment on the electrolysis of water. The paper states that by aligning the spins in a vertical direction (or parallel to electrodes I think) as much as doubles hydrogen production. It's a fairly physical reasoning that the gas bubbles are prevented from collecting on the electrodes. Otherwise, the holding of the bubbles on the electrode surface physically prevents contact with the water reducing efficiency. I guess this has already been observed, but the point is that fine-tuning the directionality as much as doubles the effect. This is way beyond a minor effect and is easily achieved. The paper also states that the introduction of the magnetic field serves to increase the current density in the electrodes, for free.

author: @ricardocalles140
text: H2 is diamagnetic and O is paramagnetic so the hydrogen component is being repelled by the lines of magnetic force while the oxygen is being attracted to the magnetic lines of force thus in essence providing a mechanical force for breaking the bonds apart.  This magnetic force supplements electrolysis from having to do all the "heavy lifting" which saves considerable current.

author: @antoniocarlossforza
text:
Adding a magnetic force produces a downward Lorentz force, and convection of the electrolyte counteracts the floating power of hydrogen bubbles and exerts a negative effect. 
Electrolyte convection is oriented in the direction of the floating power of hydrogen bubbles, positively affecting hydrogen production through electrolysis and reducing electrochemical polarization.

author: @sunsaverfromnhh9184
text: Awesome. "Magnet power" is REAL. Joe Newman died for our sins. Simply adding powerful magnets makes an electric motor STRONGER and FASTER. Joe Newman proved this, and my many motor experiments have proved this also. Now, here you are showing that this same "magnet power" effect holds true for electrochemistry as well as for electric motors: magnets actually "pull" on the electricity itself- making it more effective (in this case, producing more hydrogen and faster): or by opposition; magnets can be arranged to repel the electricity, slowing the motor, choking-off currents or repelling them, causing a motor to get hotter than it should for the applied "energy", etc. Adding magnets in one polarity makes it work way better: adding magnets in the opposite polarity makes it way worse. There is a Japanese scientist who is using pulsed magnetic fields to triple the output of his own, Brown's gas (HHO) generator, but i think the steady-state permanent magnets are good enough- use a vibrator or pumped water flow to help release bubbles from electrodes- make the plates closer together, use a better electrolyte (like strong salt water and stainless steel plates)- and now this: add permanent magnets across your capacitor plates, and the Hall effect will "speed-up" the current across the plate, without needing to raise the battery voltage or lower the electrode's electrical resistance.

Just in case anything there might be useful.

author: @AltMarc
text:
I built an electrolyser with magnets, mainly idea was to use magnetohydrodynamic to push the water and remove the bubbles on the surface of the electrodes. 15cm electrodes, 12mm surface, 5mm apart and 4 pairs of Nd magnets.
It kinda worked, I could see the water/bubble mix rise about 5mm over the surface of the water, when the electrolyser was also near the surface.
Going further with the idea, was to chain these electrolysers in a tube having circulating water...

BTW: Salty water is really bad for the magnet, they fell apart since 10 years a built that device... and I still have the same water too!

author: @ShilohSedlak
text: Now what would be awesome is if you make a video where the gasses are in a mixed container like that but on top you have 2 pipes for the gasses to travel up.. A thin square magnet sits in the middle between the 2 out going square pipes and on the outside of the tube that the North side of the magnet is facing put another square neodymium magnet with its North facing in towards the North side of the magnet in the center.. Do the same thing for the south side.. So one out going pipe is filled with a magnetic North and the other a magnetic south.. Hydrogen will go up one pipe oxygen will go up the other.. Thus you can spit the gasses without having to move the electrodes away from each other.

author: @vascovalle740
text:
amazing information and concept!

magnetism has very interesting effects on the germination of seeds.

South Pole exposure enhances seed germination rate and overall health of the seedling.

North Pole exposure stunts the growth and makes weak seedlings.

it would be very interesting to know what are (if any) the differences in effect of hydrogen product from South to North Pole exposure.

more noticeable with a decent size neodimium magnet (N45 or more strength).

thanks for your videos :)

author: @Scipiworld
text: I've observed this with some of my electrochemical cells, mainly those that produce a gaseous product. I figure it has to do with generated gas blocking portions of the electrodes. If 50% of the electrode is covered in gas bubbles, we lose 50% of our useful surface area since the ions can't travel through the gas. Adding a magnet allows the electrolyte to be stirred and knock off gas bubbles (magnetohydrodynamic effect). Similar boosts in cell performance can be achieve with stirring, electrolyte flow using a recirculating pump, or just smacking the electrode. I tend to just toss a stir bar into my cell and run under mild stirring.

replies.author: @ShilohSedlak
replies.text: Cool thing about the Magnets is no extra power input

replies.author: @Scipiworld
replies.text:
@ShilohSedlak it's not completely free. Adding the magnet makes it a form of magnetohydrodynamic pump, so work is being done on the fluid. The stirring is very small at currents used, so in this case power loss is negligible, but with careful measurement or high currents, one would likely notice a back emf generated by stirring the electrolyte this way.

Cool thing is the process works in reverse too. Flowing a conductive within a magnetic field over two plates can generate a voltage. The efficiency isn't great, but it's possible to convert a flowing conductive fluid into electric energy without any moving parts other than the fluid. The effect is very noticeable with highly conductive fluids such as mercury or galinstan.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: it could also have something to do with the water being attracted to magnets.  the water moving towards it pushes bubbles off.

replies.author: @Scipiworld
replies.text:
@ancapftw9113 I suppose that could be part of it. Water is diamagnetic, so it is repelled from any magnetic field. The effect is very weak, but and easily be observed.

A demonstration of this easy to try at home if you want something fun to do. Get two syringes filled half way with water, use glue or tape to fasten the plunger ends together, then suspend the pair of syringes with a string attached to the middle. The apparatus, if balanced, will spin away from a magnet.
